hits.title: Our bootstrapped AI-startup revenue reveal for past 4 years

story_text.value:
As an outsider to the US ecosystem, a lot of the surrounding startup activity feels strange to me. Many companies seem to be building thin wrappers, agent orchestration layers, AI-generated apps, or B2B tools whose main customers appear to be other AI startups.

At the same time, the social and funding environment seems extremely intense. Everyone is excited by “solving a problem,” everyone is racing toward YC or seed funding like it’s the new Stanford acceptance letter.

Do people inside this SF ecosystem not see how weird this looks, or is there something I’m missing? Is it just peer illusion, where everyone is surrounded by people building, funding, and hyping the same things until it starts to feel real? Or am I wrong, and the future really is DevOps agents, AI SDRs, browser operators, vibe-coded internal tools, and agent slop apps selling to other agent slop app companies?

Right now, a lot of it looks circular to me: AI startups selling vibe-codeable-in-two-days products to AI startups so they can build more tools for AI startups. The revenue frontier for this space mostly seems to be coming from this aspect, which continues to eat up the investment money, which will be drained at some point.

What are your thoughts?

title.value: Ask HN: What is happening with the current AI startup ecosystem?

story_text.value:
We're a bootstrapped AI startup doing $350K+ revenue and already spending 4-figures monthly on GCP, with our spend growing.

But, because we haven't taken venture funding, we were only approved for $2K in credits instead of the $350K for AI startups that includes Gemini credits.

This feels backwards and frankly regressive; we're a paying startup customer with real revenue, just no institutional money, and Google is going to punish us for that?

I know Azure etc will gladly throw tons of credits and inference our way to switch and save us a ton of money, but switching would be a huge distraction for us for us right now.

However, we go in and out of profitability right now, and I'm self-funding, so even the $2-3K we'd currently save each month would help us stay at break-even so I don't have to dip into my personal bank account each time we're in the red -- and there's not much left in that bank account tbh. Basically, I feel stuck.

Two questions:

- Has anyone gotten an exception as a bootstrapped company? - Has anyone just... gotten a small check from a friendly investor to technically qualify? Like could I have a VC friend write a $5K SAFE and suddenly be eligible? I like me a good loophole but while this would be an annoying distraction, it's far less annoying than having to migrate off Google entirely.

title.value: Ask HN: Has anyone gotten $200K+ GCP credits bootstrapped?

story_text.value:
Hi HN,

Recently, OpenAI has begun cracking down on the usage of "GPT" as part of startup names, citing it as a violation of their brand guidelines. This is being enforced by a company called BrandShield, who argue that this naming strategy can lead to consumer confusion.

An example of a company affected by this is SiteGPT.ai. The use of "GPT" in the name served to communicate clearly the nature of the startup - a fact reflected by their impressive growth rate. In less than a month, SiteGPT was able to achieve a $10k MRR, significantly outperforming competitors such as getchunky.io or chatbase.co.

However, in light of OpenAI's new policy, it seems we'll need to rethink our naming strategies. I'm curious about the you all's thoughts on alternative labels for GPT-inspired tooling. Here are a few of my own ideas:

* LLM: SiteLLM - LLM, probably too technical? * Bot: SiteBot - Inspired by chatbots, maybe people will confuse it with incumbent tech? * Agent: SiteAgent - Inspired by LangChain agents, maybe too technical?

Let's brainstorm together to create effective, compliant branding for our AI startups.

(Note: The revenue numbers mentioned above were shared publicly by the creators of the respective companies.)

title.value: Ask HN: Alternatives to “GPT” in Branding AI Startups?

story_text.value:
Hi HN,

I’m Vijay (founder @ RevMax AI) — we’re building a “Revenue OS” for AI agent startups. I’ve spent the past year working on flexible billing infrastructure and want to battle-test our new SDK in the real world.

What I’m offering (totally free, no catch):

I’ll build a complete billing flow for your product in the next 24 hours:

Event metering → pricing logic → Stripe (or your gateway) invoicing

Basic cost + margin dashboard

Sample tests + clean README for easy handoff

Node/TypeScript or Python (your call), PostgreSQL, Stripe Connect by default

All code is yours — open-sourced under MIT if helpful to others.

Why I’m doing this:

Real-world > sandbox tests

Want to prove how fast + flexible billing should be

If it works for you, awesome. If not, I learn.

How to get picked:

Comment here or email me: vijay [at] userevmax.com with:

What your product does

One pricing/billing edge case you're stuck on

I’ll post progress + a retro tomorrow.

story_text.value:
Been working on an idea where people can use their data to invest in AI startups.

A lot of the platforms (MTurk) out there are geared towards getting companies a buttload of low quality data from workers on the platform, who don't get paid what's worth for their data.

We want to try and flip this concept on its head - basically, people can not only rent and sell their data to AI startups, but also have the ability to invest them as a % of profits for future AI products - earn % of profits directly attributable to the company's model (all relevant AI products, services) for some (ex: 4-5 yr) period once the company earns revenue, with options to profitably contribute more. For most cases, it will probably be some combo of the three.

Similar idea goes for research ($$ on any end product it'll be used for, particularly nice if open source) -- y'all just have to add some data and you can have a stake in an AI company, while they can source the data without much upfront liability. All companies are vetted, and can add extensions to process / verify data, and automatically accept the ones they like.

Essentially, data is the bottleneck for a lot of AI companies, and we wanted to see if we can enable y'all to help 'em build out their products while having a voice in their future LLM, art model, AI products, etc.

We chatted with a couple companies, and we felt a bit interest.

What do y'all think? Would you be down to invest your data in AI (assuming there's extensions for anonymize data, clear privacy standards, etc.)?

title.value: Data as investments in LLMs, art models, and AI startups?

story_text.value:
Hi HN,

I’m researching how early-stage AI and health-tech startups think about protecting their innovations.

Traditional patents are expensive, slow and often misaligned with how fast AI products evolve. I’m curious:

Are founders filing patents early? Are you relying on trade secrets? Publishing defensively? Not worrying about IP at all? Waiting until revenue?

We’re collecting responses through a short 60-second survey to better understand real-world behavior:

https://forms.gle/8UAytkGNfge4GKrH8

If you’d rather just comment here, that’s equally helpful.

I’m happy to share aggregated insights back with the community.

Thanks, Shaheen

title.value: Ask HN: How are early-stage AI startups thinking about IP protection?

story_text.value:
GenesisAI is raising money through a crowdfunding campaign[1] and notably, only three months after raising $908k from a different crowdfunding platform[2].

The founder is telling investors the "revenue target for 2024 is $100M and $200M for 2025."

Can any AI/ML experts comment on their stated business model(s) below and if you believe it is viable $200m business based on a 30% marketplace fee?

- “We would like to lay a foundation for the creation of Artificial General Intelligence and smash the current oligopolistic system of a few large companies basically owning all the AI.”

- "Bringing AIs from all around the world to defeat COVID and get humanity back on track."

- "A global AI network that just works"

- "GenesisAI is a Machine Learning protocol. On top of this protocol, we are building a marketplace for AI products and services - an Amazon for AI services. The marketplace, which includes a built-in reputation system, connects companies in need of AI services, data, and models with companies interested in monetizing their AI tech. Initially, GenesisAI is focused on asset management space and recently entered the healthcare sector."

(all quotes from the NetCapital link)

[1] https://netcapital.com/companies/genesisai

[2] https://wefunder.com/genesis.ai

story_text.value:
Hi HN

I’m Vijay Gorfad (3× CTO turned founder). After wrestling with margin leaks at multiple AI-agent startups,where I built billing stacks and outcome-based pricing engines finally I launched RevMax, an end-to-end “Revenue OS” for AI-agent businesses. We’re live with 3 pilot partners.

What RevMax does: Launch any pricing model (outcome, usage, tiered, hybrid) in minutes—zero redeploys Pinpoint every AI cost (LLM tokens, GPU-seconds, vector DB, TTS/STT, telephony) at the task level Visualize real-time margins per agent, workflow, customer, even per API call Automate order-to-cash: invoicing, dunning, global payments, ASC 606-compliant RevRec

Early results → pilots see an avg 22% margin lift in 30 days. We’ve run 50+ founder interviews and iterated fast on feedback.

Now onboarding 25 pilot partners for our next wave of early adopters. Get instant sandbox access, a free margin audit, and help shape our roadmap.

https://userevmax.com

I’d love your feedback and to partner with 25 HN founders—drop a comment or DM me!

Vijay Gorfad Founder @ RevMax AI

story_text.value:
Hello HN,

I built an MVP of a chatbot widget for websites over the last six months (worked by weekends). The core idea: it automatically collects insights from real visitors from conversation and behavior - the things GA4 and similar tools don’t show. Moreover, it can execute tasks based on a specific behavior (visited some page, asked something specific...).

Tested on two live websites in Aug '25 (online education and consulting): ~300 conversations + ~1,000 insights. It surfaced issues like browser-specific UX bugs, incorrect information, frequent unanswered questions, missing content on first touch, etc.

I don’t have time to maintain or promote it, so I'm selling.

*Price: $15k.*

What it does: - the widget initiates conversations on specific pages (checkout, onboarding, product pages) - each session is analyzed afterwards - insights are grouped by similarity - you see why drop-offs and problems happen, not just how often - parses products and shows to visitors if they ask to do so or its tasked with - no babysitting with business information - everything is collected / updated automatically

Example use cases: - understand reasons for cart abandonment - reduce churn in SaaS via contextual help on key pages - upsell related products when visitors browse

Average conversation cost for the business is ~ $0.01 (LLM usage optimized).

Tech: Python, FastAPI, Qdrant, Redis, Postgres, Vue3, Stripe, OpenAI.

Included in sale: - domain whilio.com - full codebase - deployment help - 20 hours of support (Q&A)

Revenue is $0 - I didn’t launch paid plans due to lack of time on continuous support and development.

If interested, ask anything here or email maks@vun.one.

title.value: Ask HN: Who wants to buy an AI SaaS startup?

story_text.value:
I got tired of "I analyzed X startups with ChatGPT" posts that are pure fiction, so I did a real analysis with actual data sources.

Data: Crunchbase, Kaggle datasets (2024-2025), Mercury 2025 Report (1,500 companies), academic research (7,000+ startups), and industry benchmarks from CloudZero, KeyBanc Capital, G-Squared CFO.

Tool: Built using DashAI (our BI platform) - uploaded CSV, asked questions in natural language, generated interactive charts.

Key findings:

1. Average startup revenue: $1.2M (range $0-$10M) 2. FinTech dominates: $5.5M avg vs $800K for retail (7x!) 3. Funding paradox: More capital often = worse margins 4. The $1M revenue plateau is real (40% stuck here) 5. Operating expense variance is huge (3x difference for same revenue)

Full article includes: - All data sources (transparent methodology) - Interactive charts - Industry deep dives (FinTech, SaaS, AI/ML, DevOps, Blockchain) - 2025-2026 predictions

https://get-dashai.com/news/9?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=startup-analysis&utm_content=intro-cta

Happy to answer questions about methodology or specific findings.

title.value: Startup Analysis 2025: Real Data from 50 Companies (Not AI Fiction)

story_text.value:
Launching TeardownHQ: a directory of indie startups with revenue pulled from real signals (and labeled where it is an estimate), plus deep teardowns/playbooks of exactly how each one grew. The channels, the pricing, the GTM, all sourced, instead of the usual self-reported screenshots. Why I am building it: Starter Story proved people want "how they actually did it," but it skews ecommerce and general business, and because it is human-interviewed it is slow and broad. I am going narrow and current: indie SaaS, micro-SaaS, dev and AI tools, and no-code wins. The teardowns come out of a research pipeline with a verification layer that cuts anything it cannot source, so I can go deep and stay current without it becoming a content farm. How it works: the directory and a teardown teaser are free, which is also my SEO and sharing layer. The full teardowns are the paid product. Founders can verify they run a startup, write their own teardown, and earn a cut when it sells. The GTM I am running, since this community likes the mechanics:

- Skipped AppSumo and the LTD marketplaces on purpose (30 to 70 percent cut, and deal hunters are not my ICP).

- Building the list. Contemplating releasing 1 free playbook a week to the list pre-launch

- A capped founding-member lifetime deal at launch, then it moves to monthly.

I believe the AI-research angle is the biggest risk, mainly due to trust. I hand verify everything and have a strong hand in writing all of the reports. I have been working on this workflow for over a year and found it to be tremendously helpful but I expect skepticism from people due to the influx of AI slop that exists right now.

First teardown is live: Puff Count, the $40K/month no-code quit-vaping app that won on organic TikTok and then exited. Get the teardown at TeardownHQ.io. I would love feedback on the teardown and to know which SaaS/micro-Saas startups I should target next!

title.value: Show HN: TeardownHQ, teardowns/playbooks of how indie startups grew

How it works: the directory and a teardown teaser are free, which is also my SEO and sharing layer. The full teardowns are the paid product. Founders can verify they run a startup, write their own teardown, and earn a cut when it sells. The GTM I am running, since this community likes the mechanics:

- Skipped AppSumo and the LTD marketplaces on purpose (30 to 70 percent cut, and deal hunters are not my ICP).

- Building the list. Contemplating releasing 1 free playbook a week to the list pre-launch

- A capped founding-member lifetime deal at launch, then it moves to monthly.

I believe the AI-research angle is the biggest risk, mainly due to trust. I hand verify everything and have a strong hand in writing all of the reports. I have been working on this workflow for over a year and found it to be tremendously helpful but I expect skepticism from people due to the influx of AI slop that exists right now.

First teardown is live: Puff Count, the $40K/month no-code quit-vaping app that won on organic TikTok and then exited. Get the teardown at TeardownHQ.io. I would love feedback on the teardown and to know which SaaS/micro-Saas startups I should target next!
hits.title: Show HN: TeardownHQ, teardowns/playbooks of how indie startups grew

story_text.value:
My startup is pre-revenue. Last week i was in one of the local AI Startup meet up, and attended some GTM office hours hosted by some folks from big company marketing team.

Was told Go to market strategy is necessary, need professional helps, it will guide how i run growth campaigns(cold emailing), branding(color). and that i am not able to aquire customer maybe because i don't have a go-to-market strategy, and later on followed by a invitation for deeper GTM consultation(paid).

Because i haven't successfully gotten any customer, i cannot rule-out this possibility. But i've watched almost all YC videos on how to get first customer,

1. having a GTM is never mentioned not to mention as a mandate

2. in fact, from one of the Aaron Epstein's video, a sincere, manual, email works best, with a razor sharp ICP, this doesn't sound GTM at all, it's just doing this one, hard-coded step, no strategy is needed.

I would love to hear from you folks on your take before i either go work with these GTM folks, or follow the linear, hardcore step that YC/others have recommended.

Time is money thus asking for advices.

title.value: Ask HN: Do you need go-to-market strategy at early stage?

story_text.value:
TL;DR: Sollutin helps educational businesses to significantly lower operational costs by automating the manual work of tutors and coordinators with personalized AI learning companion.

Problem For online learning companies, a significant portion of their operational expenses—up to 80%—is consumed by the costs associated with coordinators and tutors. This heavy financial burden limits their capacity to scale and innovate, as a substantial part of their revenue is tied up in human resources dedicated to guiding and supporting students. The challenge lies not only in the high costs but also in managing the efficiency and effectiveness of these educational facilitators, ensuring they provide value that justifies their expense.

Solution Sollutin introduces an AI studying companion designed to enhance the online learning experience by engaging students in a dynamic learning process. Key features include: * Trained on your materials: The AI learns from the platform's educational materials, ensuring its guidance is rooted in the curriculum. * Doesn't just give the answers: It encourages students to uncover solutions themselves through constructive dialogue. * Human support on standby: In instances where the AI's explanations don't fully resonate, students can call for a human tutor into the conversation.

Team We’re Max and Nikita.

Nikita founded an online school in 2022 with over 100 tutors and worked as SDR in AI startup.

Max worked as ML engineer and built a bot that matched students with tutors for edtech company.

Our ask - Intros to educational companies. Please, email nikita@sollutin.com. - Book a demo at sollutin.com. - We're eager to understand your needs and show you how Sollutin can revolutionize your business costs. Here’s a Calendly link for a short 15 min convo: https://calendly.com/nik-belov/15min

title.value: Show HN: Sollutin – AI studying companion for online schools

story_text.value:
There’s no shortage of AI announcements, startups, and benchmarks but less clarity on where it’s making a meaningful difference in practice.

What’s the most concrete, sustained value you’ve seen AI create? Modest examples are welcome.

Could be:

Better outcomes for customers or users

Work that’s now possible (or enjoyable) that wasn’t before

Scientific, educational, or social impact

Revenue, margin, or productivity gains

title.value: Ask HN: What's the most real value you've seen AI create so far?
